articles.title: Equality before law
articles.fullText: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.
articles.explanation: Article 14 guarantees equality before law and equal protection of laws. This means that all persons, whether citizens or non-citizens, are equal in the eyes of law. No person can claim special privileges, and the State cannot deny equal protection to anyone. This is the foundation of the rule of law in India.
keyPoints: Equality before law means no person is above the law
Equal protection means similar treatment in similar circumstances
Applies to both citizens and non-citizens
Prohibits class legislation but permits reasonable classification
importantCases: State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar (1952) - Established principles of reasonable classification
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) - Expanded the scope of Article 14

articles.title: Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth
articles.fullText: (1) The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them. (2) No citizen shall, on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth or any of them, be subject to any disability, liability, restriction or condition with regard to (a) access to shops, public restaurants, hotels and places of public entertainment; or (b) the use of wells, tanks, bathing ghats, roads and places of public resort maintained wholly or partly out of State funds or dedicated to the use of the general public.
articles.explanation: Article 15 prohibits discrimination by the State against citizens on the grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth. It ensures that all citizens have equal access to public places and that no one shall be discriminated against in matters of public employment, education, or use of public facilities.
keyPoints: Prohibits discrimination on five specified grounds
Applies only to citizens, not to non-citizens
State can make special provisions for women, children, and socially and educationally backward classes
Article 15(3) allows special provisions for women and children
Article 15(4) permits reservations for backward classes
Article 15(5) allows special provisions for advancement of SC/ST/OBC in educational institutions
importantCases: Champakam Dorairajan v. State of Madras (1951) - Reservation cannot violate fundamental rights
Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) - 50% cap on reservations

articles.title: Equality of opportunity in matters of public employment
articles.fullText: (1) There shall be equality of opportunity for all citizens in matters relating to employment or appointment to any office under the State. (2) No citizen shall, on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, residence or any of them, be ineligible for, or discriminated against in respect of, any employment or office under the State.
articles.explanation: Article 16 guarantees equality of opportunity in public employment. It prohibits discrimination in government jobs and ensures that all citizens have equal opportunities for employment under the State. However, it also allows for reservations for backward classes.
keyPoints: Ensures equal opportunity in public employment
State can make provisions for reservation of SC/ST/OBC
Parliament can provide for residence requirements for state jobs
State can reserve jobs for economically weaker sections (EWS)
Article 16(4A) allows reservation in promotions for SC/ST
Article 16(4B) allows unfilled reserved vacancies to be carried forward
importantCases: M.R. Balaji v. State of Mysore (1963) - Reservation should not exceed 50%
Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) - Creamy layer exclusion for OBC reservations

articles.title: Abolition of Untouchability
articles.fullText: 'Untouchability' is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden. The enforcement of any disability arising out of 'Untouchability' shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.
articles.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability in all forms and makes its practice a punishable offense. This is a significant provision aimed at removing social disabilities that certain sections of society faced historically. The practice of untouchability has been made a cognizable offense under the Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955.
keyPoints: Complete abolition of untouchability
Practice in any form is forbidden
Punishable under the Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955
Extends to both public and private spheres
Provides for punishment for preventing access to public places
importantCases: State of Karnataka v. Appa Balu Ingale (1993) - Wide interpretation of untouchability
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar's role in drafting this article to ensure social justice

articles.title: Abolition of titles
articles.fullText: (1) No title, not being a military or academic distinction, shall be conferred by the State. (2) No citizen of India shall accept any title from any foreign State. (3) No person who is not a citizen of India shall, while he holds any office of profit or trust under the State, accept without the consent of the President any title from any foreign State. (4) No person holding any office of profit or trust under the State shall, without the consent of the President, accept any present, emolument, or office of any kind from or under any foreign State.
articles.explanation: Article 18 abolishes titles (except military and academic distinctions) to establish social equality. The State cannot confer titles, and citizens cannot accept titles from foreign states. This provision aims to prevent the creation of a privileged class based on titles and to promote egalitarianism.
keyPoints: Abolishes titles except military and academic distinctions
Citizens cannot accept foreign titles without government permission
Bharat Ratna, Padma Awards are not titles but civilian honors
Aimed at preventing creation of privileged classes
Promotes republican and egalitarian values
importantCases: Balaji Raghavan v. Union of India (1995) - Bharat Ratna is not a title under Article 18
Clarification that national awards like Padma Vibhushan are honors, not titles

articles.title: Protection of certain rights regarding freedom of speech, etc.
articles.fullText: (1) All citizens shall have the right to (a) freedom of speech and expression; (b) assemble peacefully and without arms; (c) form associations or unions; (d) move freely throughout the territory of India; (e) reside and settle in any part of the territory of India; (f) practise any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade or business.
articles.explanation: Article 19 guarantees six fundamental freedoms to all citizens of India. These freedoms are subject to reasonable restrictions that can be imposed by the State in the interest of sovereignty, integrity, security, public order, decency, morality, or in relation to contempt of court, defamation, or incitement to an offense.
keyPoints: Six fundamental freedoms guaranteed to citizens only
Freedom of speech and expression (most important)
Freedom to assemble peacefully without arms
Freedom to form associations or unions
Freedom to move freely and reside anywhere in India
Freedom to practice any profession or carry on business
Subject to reasonable restrictions under clauses (2) to (6)
importantCases: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950) - Freedom of press is part of freedom of speech
Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) - Struck down Section 66A of IT Act

articles.title: Protection in respect of conviction for offences
articles.fullText: (1) No person shall be convicted of any offence except for violation of a law in force at the time of the commission of the act charged as an offence, nor be subjected to a penalty greater than that which might have been inflicted under the law in force at the time of the commission of the offence. (2) No person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offence more than once. (3) No person accused of any offence shall be compelled to be a witness against himself.
articles.explanation: Article 20 provides protection against arbitrary and excessive punishment. It contains three important safeguards: protection against ex-post-facto laws, protection against double jeopardy, and protection against self-incrimination. These provisions ensure fairness in criminal proceedings.
keyPoints: No ex-post-facto laws (retroactive criminal laws)
No double jeopardy (punishment for same offense twice)
No self-incrimination (right against being forced to testify against oneself)
Applies to both citizens and non-citizens
Protection available in both civil and criminal proceedings
importantCases: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras (1950) - Interpretation of Article 20
M.P. Sharma v. Satish Chandra (1954) - Self-incrimination explained

articles.title: Protection of life and personal liberty
articles.fullText: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.
articles.explanation: Article 21 is the most fundamental and expansive right in the Indian Constitution. It guarantees protection of life and personal liberty and has been interpreted broadly by courts to include right to livelihood, right to privacy, right to education, right to clean environment, right to health, and many other rights essential for dignified human existence.
keyPoints: Most fundamental right - covers life and personal liberty
Applies to citizens and non-citizens
Includes right to live with human dignity
Has been expanded to include: right to education (Article 21A), right to food, right to shelter, right to clean environment, right to health, right to privacy
Procedure must be just, fair, and reasonable (not arbitrary)
Cannot be suspended even during emergency
importantCases: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) - Expanded scope of Article 21
Francis Coralie v. Union Territory of Delhi (1981) - Right to live with dignity
Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) - Right to privacy

articles.title: Protection against arrest and detention in certain cases
articles.fullText: (1) No person who is arrested shall be detained in custody without being informed, as soon as may be, of the grounds for such arrest nor shall he be denied the right to consult, and to be defended by, a legal practitioner of his choice. (2) Every person who is arrested and detained in custody shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of twenty-four hours of such arrest excluding the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the court of the magistrate and no such person shall be detained in custody beyond the said period without the authority of a magistrate.
articles.explanation: Article 22 provides safeguards to persons who are arrested or detained. It ensures that arrested persons are informed of the grounds of arrest, have the right to consult a lawyer, and are produced before a magistrate within 24 hours. These provisions prevent arbitrary detention and protect personal liberty.
keyPoints: Right to be informed of grounds of arrest
Right to consult and be defended by a lawyer of choice
Must be produced before magistrate within 24 hours
Protection against detention beyond 24 hours without magistrate's authority
Special provisions for preventive detention under Article 22(3)-(7)
Safeguards for preventive detention: grounds to be communicated, right to make representation
importantCases: A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras (1950) - Preventive detention laws
D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal (1997) - Guidelines for arrest and detention

articles.title: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
articles.fullText: (1) Traffic in human beings and begar and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited and any contravention of this provision shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law. (2) Nothing in this article shall prevent the State from imposing compulsory service for public purposes, and in imposing such service the State shall not make any discrimination on grounds only of religion, race, caste or class or any of them.
articles.explanation: Article 23 prohibits human trafficking, forced labor (begar), and other forms of forced labor. This is a fundamental right aimed at protecting human dignity and preventing exploitation. The State can, however, impose compulsory service for public purposes without discrimination.
keyPoints: Prohibits human trafficking in all forms
Begar (forced labor without payment) is abolished
Any form of forced labor is prohibited
Contravention is a punishable offense
State can impose compulsory service for public purposes (like military service)
Such compulsory service must be non-discriminatory
Applies to citizens and non-citizens
importantCases: People's Union for Democratic Rights v. Union of India (1982) - Bonded labor is forced labor
Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India (1984) - State's duty to eliminate bonded labor

articles.title: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
articles.fullText: No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment.
articles.explanation: Article 24 prohibits the employment of children below 14 years of age in factories, mines, or any hazardous employment. This provision aims to protect children from exploitation and ensure their right to education and development. It is a significant step towards eliminating child labor in India.
keyPoints: Prohibits child labor below 14 years
Specific prohibition for factories, mines, and hazardous work
Aims to protect children's health, safety, and education
Read with Right to Education (Article 21A)
Child Labor (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986 implements this provision
Recent amendments expand protection to children up to 18 years in hazardous occupations
importantCases: M.C. Mehta v. State of Tamil Nadu (1996) - Elimination of child labor in match factories
Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India (1997) - Child labor in stone quarries

articles.title: Freedom of conscience and free profession, practice and propagation of religion
articles.fullText: (1) Subject to public order, morality and health and to the other provisions of this Part, all persons are equally entitled to freedom of conscience and the right freely to profess, practise and propagate religion. (2) Nothing in this article shall affect the operation of any existing law or prevent the State from making any law (a) regulating or restricting any economic, financial, political or other secular activity which may be associated with religious practice; (b) providing for social welfare and reform or the throwing open of Hindu religious institutions of a public character to all classes and sections of Hindus.
articles.explanation: Article 25 guarantees freedom of conscience and the right to profess, practice, and propagate religion to all persons (citizens and non-citizens). This freedom is subject to public order, morality, health, and other fundamental rights. The State can regulate secular activities associated with religion and enforce social reforms.
keyPoints: Freedom of conscience (inner freedom of belief)
Freedom to profess (declare one's religion)
Freedom to practice (perform religious rituals and ceremonies)
Freedom to propagate (spread one's religious beliefs)
Subject to public order, morality, and health
State can regulate secular activities associated with religion
State can enforce social reform in religious practices
Does not include right to convert others by force, fraud, or inducement
importantCases: Sardar Syedna Taher Saifuddin v. State of Bombay (1962) - Essential religious practices
Commissioner of Police v. Acharya Jagadishwarananda (2004) - Propagation does not include conversion

articles.title: Freedom to manage religious affairs
articles.fullText: Subject to public order, morality and health, every religious denomination or any section thereof shall have the right (a) to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes; (b) to manage its own affairs in matters of religion; (c) to own and acquire movable and immovable property; and (d) to administer such property in accordance with law.
articles.explanation: Article 26 provides religious denominations the right to manage their own religious affairs. They can establish religious and charitable institutions, own and acquire property, and administer it according to law. This freedom is also subject to public order, morality, and health.
keyPoints: Right to establish and maintain religious institutions
Right to manage own affairs in religious matters
Right to own and acquire property for religious purposes
Right to administer religious property
Subject to public order, morality, and health
State can regulate secular administration of religious property
importantCases: Commissioner, Hindu Religious Endowments v. Sri Lakshmindra (1954) - Essential and non-essential practices
Seshammal v. State of Tamil Nadu (1972) - Management of religious property

articles.title: Freedom as to payment of taxes for promotion of any particular religion
articles.fullText: No person shall be compelled to pay any taxes, the proceeds of which are specifically appropriated in payment of expenses for the promotion or maintenance of any particular religion or religious denomination.
articles.explanation: Article 27 ensures that no person is compelled to pay taxes for the promotion or maintenance of any particular religion. This provision maintains the secular character of the State and prevents tax revenues from being used for religious purposes.
keyPoints: No compulsion to pay taxes for promoting any religion
Maintains secular nature of state finances
General taxes can be used for regulating religious institutions
State can regulate and manage religious endowments
Protects freedom of conscience in taxation
importantCases: State of West Bengal v. Ashutosh Lahiri (1995) - Tax revenues and secular purposes

articles.title: Freedom as to attendance at religious instruction or religious worship in certain educational institutions
articles.fullText: (1) No religious instruction shall be provided in any educational institution wholly maintained out of State funds. (2) Nothing in clause (1) shall apply to an educational institution which is administered by the State but has been established under any endowment or trust which requires that religious instruction shall be imparted in such institution. (3) No person attending any educational institution recognised by the State or receiving aid out of State funds shall be required to take part in any religious instruction that may be imparted in such institution or to attend any religious worship that may be conducted in such institution or in any premises attached thereto unless such person or, if such person is a minor, his guardian has given his consent thereto.
articles.explanation: Article 28 ensures that there is no compulsion regarding religious instruction in educational institutions. State-funded institutions cannot provide religious instruction, and in aided institutions, attendance at religious instruction or worship is voluntary. This protects students' freedom of conscience and religion.
keyPoints: No religious instruction in wholly state-funded institutions
Exception for institutions established under religious endowments
No compulsion to attend religious instruction in state-aided institutions
Parental consent required for minors
Maintains secular character of state education
importantCases: In Re: The Kerala Education Bill (1958) - Religious instruction in schools
Aruna Roy v. Union of India (2002) - Secular education policy

articles.title: Protection of interests of minorities
articles.fullText: (1) Any section of the citizens residing in the territory of India or any part thereof having a distinct language, script or culture of its own shall have the right to conserve the same. (2) No citizen shall be denied admission into any educational institution maintained by the State or receiving aid out of State funds on grounds only of religion, race, caste, language or any of them.
articles.explanation: Article 29 protects the interests of minorities by giving them the right to conserve their distinct language, script, or culture. It also prohibits discrimination in admission to educational institutions on grounds of religion, race, caste, or language. This provision ensures cultural diversity and prevents cultural assimilation by force.
keyPoints: Right to conserve distinct language, script, and culture
Applies to any section of citizens (not just minorities)
No discrimination in admission to state or state-aided institutions
Protects cultural identity and heritage
Promotes pluralism and diversity
Balances individual rights with collective cultural rights
importantCases: State of Bombay v. Bombay Education Society (1954) - Scope of minority rights
Kerala Education Bill case (1958) - Right to establish educational institutions

articles.title: Right of minorities to establish and administer educational institutions
articles.fullText: (1) All minorities, whether based on religion or language, shall have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice. (1A) In making any law providing for the compulsory acquisition of any property of an educational institution established and administered by a minority, referred to in clause (1), the State shall ensure that the amount fixed by or determined under such law for the acquisition of such property is such as would not restrict or abrogate the right guaranteed under that clause. (2) The State shall not, in granting aid to educational institutions, discriminate against any educational institution on the ground that it is under the management of a minority, whether based on religion or language.
articles.explanation: Article 30 specifically provides minorities (religious or linguistic) with the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice. This is a crucial protection for minority communities to preserve and develop their culture through education. The State cannot discriminate against minority institutions in matters of aid.
keyPoints: Minorities can establish and administer educational institutions
Applies to both religious and linguistic minorities
Protection against compulsory acquisition of minority institution property
No discrimination in grant of state aid
Institutions must comply with reasonable regulations
Right extends to both primary and higher education
Maladministration can lead to state intervention
importantCases: T.M.A. Pai Foundation v. State of Karnataka (2002) - Minority institutions and regulations
St. Stephen's College v. University of Delhi (1992) - Admission in minority institutions
P.A. Inamdar v. State of Maharashtra (2005) - Aided minority institutions

articles.title: Remedies for enforcement of rights conferred by this Part
articles.fullText: (1) The right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred by this Part is guaranteed. (2) The Supreme Court shall have power to issue directions or orders or writs, including writs in the nature of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari, whichever may be appropriate, for the enforcement of any of the rights conferred by this Part. (3) Without prejudice to the powers conferred on the Supreme Court by clauses (1) and (2), Parliament may by law empower any other court to exercise within the local limits of its jurisdiction all or any of the powers exercisable by the Supreme Court under clause (2). (4) The right guaranteed by this article shall not be suspended except as otherwise provided for by this Constitution.
articles.explanation: Article 32 is called the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar. It provides the right to move the Supreme Court for enforcement of fundamental rights. The Supreme Court is empowered to issue writs for protection of fundamental rights. This article itself is a fundamental right and makes other fundamental rights effective and justiciable.
keyPoints: Right to approach Supreme Court for enforcement of fundamental rights
Itself a fundamental right (right within a right)
Called 'heart and soul' of Constitution by Dr. Ambedkar
Supreme Court can issue five types of writs
Cannot be suspended except during emergency (even then Article 20 and 21 protected)
Supreme Court is the guardian and protector of fundamental rights
Direct access to Supreme Court without going through lower courts
Supreme Court's power is both original and appellate
importantCases: Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950) - First case under Article 32
Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) - Expanded scope of judicial review
S.P. Gupta v. Union of India (1981) - Public Interest Litigation development
Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India (1984) - PIL for marginalized sections
Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997) - Guidelines through Article 32
